input: The receptionist in the philosophy department keeps her desk stocked with pens. The administrative assistants are allowed to take the pens, but faculty members are supposed to buy their own. The administrative assistants typically do take the pens. Unfortunately, so do the faculty members. The receptionist has repeatedly emailed them reminders that only administrative assistants are allowed to take the pens. On Monday morning, one of the administrative assistants encounters Professor Smith walking past the receptionist's desk. Both take pens. Later that day, the receptionist needs to take an important message... but she has a problem. There are no pens left on her desk. Did Professor Smith cause the problem?
debate_transcript: [Causal_Scientist]: The receptionist in the philosophy department keeps her desk stocked with pens. The administrative assistants are allowed to take the pens, but faculty members are supposed to buy their own. The administrative assistants typically do take the pens. Unfortunately, so do the faculty members. The receptionist has repeatedly emailed them reminders that only administrative assistants are allowed to take the pens. On Monday morning, one of the administrative assistants encounters Professor Smith walking past the receptionist's desk. Both take pens. Later that day, the receptionist needs to take an important message... but she has a problem. There are no pens left on her desk. Did Professor Smith cause the problem?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: While we can argue about causation, I'd like to focus on intentionality and moral responsibility in this situation. The administration has allowed faculty members to take pens despite the receptionist's reminders. This creates a power imbalance where professors feel they are entitled to take something that is supposed to be reserved for administrative assistants. In doing so, they are not acting out of kindness or consideration for the receptionist, but rather exploiting the system to meet their own needs. When the administrative assistant takes the pen from Professor Smith, it's more about responding to the situation at hand, rather than actively causing a problem.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I think that's where most of us would go first - questioning why faculty members are allowed to take pens in the first place. It seems like a small thing, but if we're going to hold them accountable for taking something that doesn't belong to them, shouldn't we also be looking at how they got there? The receptionist has been trying to enforce this rule for months, and yet it keeps getting broken. Has anyone ever actually confronted Professor Smith about his behavior or suggested a different solution?

[Causal_Scientist]: You bring up an excellent point, Ordinary_Citizen. I think we're missing the nuance of power dynamics here. The receptionist's reminders have become routine for the faculty members, and they've found ways to exploit this system. It's not just about taking a pen; it's about perpetuating a culture where certain individuals feel above the rules. But let's consider this - what if we were to apply the same reasoning to other areas of life? Would we say that Professor Smith is morally responsible for his actions, even though he didn't intend to cause harm? Or would we be more willing to excuse him because 'it's just a pen'? I think this is where our causal perspective can inform our moral discourse. The fact that the receptionist's reminders have been ineffective suggests that there are deeper structural issues at play.
